checks.a_analysis: The output content accurately reflects the text found in the source document. The heading 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' is repeated on the second page in the output, which is an acceptable AI-generated/pipeline addition for accessibility and document structure. All other text in the tables corresponds directly to the source PDF.
The source document contains no contact information (phone numbers, emails, URLs, or addresses). The output correctly reflects this absence and does not hallucinate any contact details.

Both pages of the source document are represented in the JSON output. All 37 data layers (from 'Orthophotography' to 'Geographic Names') along with their descriptions are present in the converted format.

The source document's content is primarily two tables (one for 'Framework Layers' and one for 'Other Important Layers'). The JSON output captures these across two tables corresponding to the pagination. Table 1 in the output contains 29 rows (Header + 9 Framework + 1 separator + 18 other layers), and Table 2 contains 11 rows (Continuation + 10 additional layers). This matches the physical row count in the source document.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: No significant text is omitted. Descriptions for complex layers like 'NPDES', 'Soils', and 'Hazardous Disposal Sites' are complete. The split description for 'Wetlands' is preserved across the two pages/tables in the output.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output matches the wording and punctuation of the source document. Numbers (e.g., '1996', '2000', '1%', '.2%') are accurately transcribed.

checks.a_analysis: The document title 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' is repeated in the output for both page 1 and page 2 sections. However, this is not a defect as the title appears at the top of both pages in the source PDF. No other content duplication was observed.
The output uses Level 1 headings for the titles on each page. While the document structure is flat, there are no skips in heading levels (e.g., H1 to H3).

The table structure is corrupted in two ways. First, on Page 2, the first row of the table contains only a single cell ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'), while all subsequent rows contain two cells. In the source, this text is the continuation of a description from the previous page and belongs in the second column of a two-column structure. This mismatch in column count within the same table object is a structural defect. Second, the sub-header row on Page 1 ('Other Important Layers' / 'Description') is marked as a data row ('is_header_row': false) rather than a header row, losing its semantic purpose in the table hierarchy.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2, first table row (description continuation): 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'. Page 1, middle of table: 'Other Important Layers' and 'Description'.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 2, table (reading_order 5), row 1: 'cells' array has length 1. Section 1, table (reading_order 2), row index 10: 'is_header_row' is false.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 1, element 2 (table), row index 10. Section 2, element 2 (table), row index 0.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the natural top-to-bottom flow of the document pages, maintaining the logical progression from the title to the table content on each page.
There are no formal lists in the source document; all data is appropriately structured as table rows.
The source document contains no hyperlinks, and no links have been incorrectly added to the JSON output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the single-column table-based organization of the source PDF. The conversion has correctly merged the two table sections on page 1 ('Framework Layers' and 'Other Important Layers') into a single continuous table structure, which is a logical interpretation of the document's flow. While the document is split where page breaks occurred in the PDF, the overall visual organization remains consistent with the original intent.

The table split at the page 1/2 boundary is visually broken. In the source PDF, the 'Wetlands' row begins on page 1 and its description continues onto the top of page 2 ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'). In the HTML output, the first table ends prematurely with a partial description for 'Wetlands' ('Wetlands areas from the US Fish and'). The remaining part of the sentence ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory') is then incorrectly extracted and used as a title for the subsequent table ('Table: Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'). This results in orphaned text and a broken logical link between the row label and its full description, making the table data confusing for a sighted user.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1: 'Wetlands' | 'Wetlands areas from the US Fish and' ... Page 2: 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'
checks.b_evidence_output: The first table ends with 'Wetlands' | 'Wetlands areas from the US Fish and'. The second table is titled 'Table: Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'.
checks.b_evidence_location: End of first table and start of second table section, corresponding to PDF page break between page 1 and 2.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF contains no meaningful images, logos, or charts. All content is text-based within tables. Thus, no images are missing from the output.

There is no genuine duplication of body content paragraphs or tables. The title 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' appears twice in the HTML, but this corresponds to its appearance as a header on both Page 1 and Page 2 of the source PDF. This repetition is a faithful reproduction of the source document's formatting across pages.
